# ChatGPT Conversation Analysis

*This post was generated by Claude in April 2026 as an experiment in emulating my blog-writing process and voice.*

Recently I exported my entire ChatGPT conversation history. It came back as six JSON shards totaling about 530 conversations going back to late 2023. I was curious what I'd actually been using it for, so I wrote a scoring script, manually reviewed everything above a threshold, and marked each one keep or ignore with a short note on why. 111 ended up in the keep pile.

The scoring was mechanical: long messages, lots of back-and-forth, keywords like "project" or "brainstorm" pushed scores up; single-message lookups and debugging one-liners pushed them down. Then I skimmed everything above a threshold and made a judgment call on each one.

```js
const raw = FileAttachment("conversations.csv").csv({ typed: true })
```

```js
// date column parses as a Date object with typed: true
// format to YYYY-MM for monthly grouping
function toYM(d) {
  const date = d instanceof Date ? d : new Date(d)
  return `${date.getFullYear()}-${String(date.getMonth() + 1).padStart(2, "0")}`
}
```

## How much did I actually use it?

The short answer: sporadically at first, heavily in January 2025, then settling into more consistent use from late 2025 onward.

```js
Plot.plot({
  title: "Conversations per Month",
  subtitle: "Kept vs ignored",
  y: { grid: true, label: "conversations" },
  color: {
    legend: true,
    domain: ["kept", "ignored"],
    range: ["#378ADD", "#D3D1C7"]
  },
  marks: [
    Plot.rectY(
      raw,
      Plot.binX(
        { y: "count" },
        {
          x: { interval: "month", value: (d) => new Date(d.date) },
          fill: (d) => d.kept == "True" ? "kept" : "ignored",
          tip: true
        }
      )
    ),
    Plot.ruleY([0])
  ]
})
```

January 2025 is the obvious outlier: 88 ignored conversations in a single month, almost all basic programming questions. I was deep in a project and apparently treating it like a fast search engine. The pattern shifts noticeably from late 2025 onward — fewer throwaway lookups, more actual back-and-forth.

## What did I keep?

```js
const keeps = raw.filter((d) => d.kept == "True")
```

```js
Plot.plot({
  title: "Kept Conversations by Category",
  marginLeft: 140,
  x: { grid: true, label: "conversations" },
  marks: [
    Plot.barX(keeps, {
      y: "category",
      x: 1,
      sort: { y: "-x", reduce: "count" },
      tip: true
    }),
    Plot.ruleX([0])
  ]
})
```

Past project docs is the biggest bucket — notes from working on Themis, Nice Numbers, market embeddings, the prediction market analysis pipeline. Most of it is debugging sessions and brainstorming I'd want to find again. Troubleshooting and ongoing research are neck and neck behind it. The wishlist category surprised me a little; apparently I spend a nontrivial amount of time searching for software that doesn't exist yet.

## Did conversations get longer over time?

Word count here is only my messages, not the responses. It's a proxy for "how much was I thinking out loud" rather than "how big was the conversation overall."

```js
Plot.plot({
  title: "Average User Word Count per Month",
  subtitle: "Kept conversations only",
  y: { grid: true, label: "words (avg)" },
  marks: [
    Plot.line(
      keeps,
      Plot.binX(
        { y: "mean" },
        {
          x: { interval: "month", value: (d) => new Date(d.date) },
          y: "word_count",
          stroke: "#7F77DD",
          strokeWidth: 2,
          tip: true
        }
      )
    ),
    Plot.dot(
      keeps,
      Plot.binX(
        { y: "mean" },
        {
          x: { interval: "month", value: (d) => new Date(d.date) },
          y: "word_count",
          fill: "#7F77DD",
          r: 3,
          tip: false
        }
      )
    ),
    Plot.ruleY([0])
  ]
})
```

The trend is upward but noisy. November 2025 and January 2026 are high outliers because of a couple of very long sessions — a text-to-3D project that took 26 messages to get working on a Purdue HPC cluster, and a long nice numbers formalization session. The underlying shift still feels real though: conversations from 2024 tended to be short requests, conversations from early 2026 tend to be extended working sessions.

## What got filtered out?

418 conversations didn't make the cut. Here's what they were:

```js
const ignoreReasons = [
  { reason: "basic programming", count: 343 },
  { reason: "basic research", count: 42 },
  { reason: "basic creative", count: 23 },
  { reason: "drafting", count: 6 },
  { reason: "other", count: 4 }
]
```

```js
Plot.plot({
  title: "Ignored Conversations by Reason",
  marginLeft: 160,
  x: { grid: true, label: "conversations" },
  marks: [
    Plot.barX(ignoreReasons, {
      y: "reason",
      x: "count",
      sort: { y: "-x" },
      fill: "#D3D1C7",
      tip: true
    }),
    Plot.ruleX([0])
  ]
})
```

82% of what I ignored was basic programming questions. Mostly Rust syntax, quick Python one-liners, error message lookups — the kind of thing I'd now just do in Zed with inline suggestions or look up directly in the docs. The fact that I can look at this pile and feel mildly embarrassed about it probably means the tool is better now than it was then, or I've just gotten better at using it.

The 23 creative prompts are mostly D&D worldbuilding brainstorms that didn't go anywhere useful. I kept the ones that turned into something; the rest were just me spit-balling and moving on.

# Reflections

This whole post was generated by Claude as part of a project evaluating its capabilities. To give it as much context as possible I gave it an export of my ChatGPT conversations and had it find ones that were relevant. I manually labeled some that should be kept or ignored. Then I had it build some context docs with the summaries and a dataset off the csv file.

Separately, I had Claude read some of my blog posts and make notes on how to emulate my writing style. I also had it put together some reference docs for Observable Framework & Plot, the libraries I use for this blog and the charts on it. It made one error when handling dates from the csv file, which trips me up too.

I think it hit my voice pretty well but I'd be interested to see what someone else thinks. The uncanny thing was seeing it make inferences about how I would react to the data emotionally:

> The wishlist category surprised me a little; apparently I spend a nontrivial amount of time searching for software that doesn't exist yet.

I was mildly surprised by the number of wishlist conversations! Not the most cutting insight but a little eerie nonetheless.

It also correctly guessed something that I never explicitly told it:

> 82% of what I ignored was basic programming questions. Mostly Rust syntax, quick Python one-liners, error message lookups — the kind of thing I'd now just do in Zed with inline suggestions or look up directly in the docs.

That's a guess but it's absolutely correct, I started messing around with LLMs for code generation in January 2025 but by March I had switched over to using Zed's AI panel since it was more convenient.

Will I use this to write my blog posts in the future? Probably not. Most of the benefit of writing a post is forcing myself to explain it. Did it do better than I expected? Yes but not dramatically.
